Title: How to Change the Notification Sound on Your iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the notification sound on your iPhone. This can be helpful if you want to personalize your device or make it easier to differentiate between various types of alerts. By following these steps, you'll be able to customize your notification sounds with ease. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Make sure your iPhone is updated to the latest iOS version before proceeding. - Be aware that some apps may not allow you to change their individual notification sounds due to app restrictions. Step 1: Unlock Your iPhone To begin, unlock your iPhone using Face ID, Touch ID, or your passcode. This allows access to the home screen where settings are located. Step 2: Access Settings App Find and tap on the gray gear icon labeled 'Settings'. The Settings app contains all the configurations available for adjustment within your iPhone. Step 3: Navigate to Sounds & Haptics Scroll down until you find 'Sounds & Haptics', then select this option. Here, you're given control over numerous auditory aspects of your iPhone such as ringtones, text tones, new mail, and more. Step 4: Choose Specific Sound Category Under the 'Sounds and Vibration Patterns' section, choose which type of alert you wish to modify by selecting one of the categories (e.g., Ringtone, Text Tone, New Mail). For our example, we'll pick 'Text Tone'. Step 5: Select Desired Sound Once inside the chosen category, you'll see a list of preinstalled sounds along with any additional ones downloaded from iTunes Store. To preview a sound, simply tap on its name. After deciding upon a preferred tone, ensure it's selected so there's a checkmark next to it. Step 6: Confirm Changes Navigate back to the main menu of the 'Sounds & Haptics' page either via the top left arrow button or scrolling upwards and clicking 'Back' at the top. Check whether the changed setting appears under 'SOUNDS AND VIBRATION PATTERNS'. If correct, exit out of Settings. Additional Steps for Downloaded Sounds: If none of the provided default options suit your taste, consider downloading extra sounds from the iTunes Store. Follow these bonus steps to do so: Step A: Open App Store From your home screen, locate and open the blue App Store icon. Step B: Search for 'Sounds' Within the search bar at the bottom, type 'Sounds' and hit 'Search'. Amongst the results, look for free or paid apps offering customizable notification sounds. Popular examples include Zedge and Sounds. Step C: Download Preferred Sound Pack Choose an application that appeals to you, browse through its offerings, and download a suitable pack containing multiple sounds or just single ones. Keep in mind certain applications might require in-app purchases for premium content. Step D: Import Selected Sound After obtaining the desired audio files, head back to 'Sounds & Haptics' > '(Category Name)' > and tap 'Downloads' or 'Purchased' tab near the top; depending on the app used. From here, select your newly acquired sound and set it as your preference. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have now successfully altered your iPhone's notification sound according to your liking. With regular updates and exploration, you can continue discovering new ways to tailor your device experience further. That means you every mother would like to ride on you WWW [laughs]. Snowy owls live in the Arctic Tundra. This includes places like north of North America, Greenland, north of Europe and Asia. When breeding season comes, snowy owls remain in the Arctic Tundra. These owls have white feathers with light gray spots. The white feather protects the snowy owl from the cold and camouflages them against enemies. When they spread their wings, they could reach up to 4 -5 feet (1.2 - 1.5m) wide. The snowy owls prey on lemmings for most of the time. However, on the some occasion, they feed on larger preys like hares, gulls, and ducks. During years when food supply is scarce, these owls migrate south to search for food. The nests of snowy owls are shallow and hollow holes in the ground. They are usually located on the slightly raised ground on the tundra floor. The average clutch size is 8 - 15 eggs. Each egg needs 32 -33 days of incubation. They hatch in 2 - 4 intervals. Once there was a young girl named Maria. She was only three years old and full of energy! The driver gave her a big can of the fuel. Maria took the can and thanked the driver. She ran back to her garden full of excitement and opened her can. Title: How to Call a Private Number in Mexico Using an iPhone or iPad Introduction: Making private calls can be necessary for various reasons such as maintaining privacy, avoiding unwanted callers, or conducting confidential business transactions. This tutorial will guide you through the process of making private calls from your iPhone or iPad to any number within Mexico. Please note that while this article focuses on calling Mexican numbers, the steps are applicable when dialing internationally from iOS devices. Key Tips and Guidelines: - The method described here uses the '*67' prefix before the phone number to make a private call. - Making private calls may not work with all carriers due to different policies regarding anonymous calls. It is essential to check if your carrier supports this feature. - Be aware that using *67 might still display 'Private', 'Blocked', or similar labels on some recipients' caller IDs depending upon their service provider. - Misuse of private calling features could lead to account suspension; hence always ensure responsible usage. Step 1: Open the Phone App On your home screen, locate the greenPhone app icon. Tap on it to open. If you cannot find the app, swipe down on any screen and type "Phone" into the search bar at the top of the page. Step 2: Navigate to the Keypad Screen Once you have opened the Phone app, look towards the bottom right corner where you will see a keypad symbol (it looks like a traditional telephone keypad). Press this button to access the dialer pad. Step 3: Dial the Prefix (*67) At the beginning of the number you wish to call privately, enter the star character (*), then the number six (6), followed by another star character (*). For example, if you want to call the number +521234567890, you would input *67+521234567890. Step 4: Understand What Happens When You Use *67 The "*67" code tells your wireless carrier to block your phone number from being displayed to the recipient. However, keep in mind that results vary based on the recipient's phone carrier since not all support full hiding of incoming calls. Step 5: Enter Recipient's Number After entering the *67 prefix, proceed by typing the full Mexican phone number you intend to contact. Remember, for international calls, include the country code (+52 for Mexico) preceded by the plus sign (+). Step 6: Initiate the Call Having correctly entered the recipient's number after the *67 prefix, press the green 'call' button located at the bottom right side of your screen. Your device will start connecting the call anonymously. Step 7: Confirm Anonymous Status During the Call During the call, listen carefully for confirmation messages indicating whether your call has been successfully made private. Some phones play a tone or give verbal confirmation. Unfortunately, others do not provide feedback, so rely on the recipient confirming they see 'Private,' 'Anonymous,' etc., on their display. Conclusion: In conclusion, dialling *67 prior to the intended number allows users to mask their identity during outbound calls. While effectiveness varies between carriers, following these steps ensures maximum likelihood of successful private communication. Always adhere to ethical practices when utilizing these functions. Thomas you're banging me and the tape recorder okay? Title: Celebrate Christmas This Year Introduction: Christmas is a joyous holiday celebrated around the world. It's a time for family, friends, gift-giving, and cherishing traditions. Whether you celebrate Christmas as a religious holiday or simply enjoy the festive atmosphere, this tutorial will provide you with comprehensive steps to help make your celebration memorable. Step 1: Decorating Your Home Decorating your home sets the mood for the season. Start by putting up a Christmas tree, either real or artificial. Real trees can be sourced from local farms or markets, while artificial ones are available at various retail stores. Choose decorations that reflect your personal taste - ornaments, lights, tinsel, and garlands. Key tip: Make sure all electrical items used for decoration comply with safety standards. Step 2: Preparing Festive Food Food plays an essential role in most celebrations. For Christmas, traditional dishes may include roast turkey, ham, stuffing, mashed potatoes, gravy, cranberry sauce, and pies (like pumpkin or pecan). However, feel free to incorporate cultural twists based on your background or preferences. Guidelines: Plan meals ahead of time considering dietary restrictions of guests, if any. Consider making some dishes beforehand and freezing them to save time during the busy holiday season. Step 3: Exchanging Gifts Gift-giving is a significant part of many people's Christmas tradition. Determine who you're buying gifts for and set a budget early on. Brainstorm ideas according to recipients' interests. Remember, thoughtful gifts needn't always be expensive; handmade presents or tokens representing shared memories can also be meaningful. Tip: Keep track of what you buy to avoid duplicates and ensure everyone receives something. Step 4: Engaging in Holiday Activities Engage in activities that spread cheer and enhance the spirit of the season. These could range from carol singing, watching classic Christmas movies, attending church services, volunteering at community centers, to creating homemade crafts. Such activities foster bonding and create lasting memories. Suggestion: Try incorporating new activities every year to keep things exciting. Step 5: Spending Quality Time With Loved Ones The essence of Christmas lies in spending quality time with those we care about. Arrange gatherings with family and friends, play games, share stories, cook together, go ice skating, have snowball fights (if weather permits), etc. The goal is to strengthen relationships and build new ones. Important note: Be inclusive when planning events so no one feels left out. Step 6: Reflecting on the True Meaning of Christmas For Christians, Christmas marks the birth of Jesus Christ. Regardless of faith, it provides an opportunity to reflect on values like love, peace, kindness, forgiveness, and hope. Take time individually or collectively to meditate upon these themes through prayer, reading scriptures, engaging in quiet reflection, or participating in group discussions. By doing so, you enrich both yourself and your community. Conclusion: By following these steps, you'll successfully create a heartwarming and enjoyable Christmas experience. While there might be challenges along the way - financial constraints, disagreements among relatives, stress related to preparations - remember that the true spirit of Christmas transcends these issues. Stay positive, flexible, and open-minded throughout the process. After all, the goal isn't perfection but rather fostering connection, expressing gratitude, and cultivating happiness. Title: How to Prepare for Your First Cheer Competition Introduction: Participating in your first cheer competition can be both exciting and nerve-wracking. It requires dedication, hard work, and proper preparation. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the steps needed to get ready for your debut cheer competition, ensuring that you feel confident, prepared, and excited to showcase your skills. Step 1: Understand the Rules and Regulations Explanation: Before starting any training or choreography, familiarize yourself with the rules and regulations specific to your competition. Each event may have different requirements regarding uniforms, music, stunts, tumbling, and safety measures. Knowing these details early on allows you to plan accordingly and avoid potential disqualification. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Review the official rulebook provided by the competition organizers. * Attend informational meetings or clinics offered by the competition hosts. * Consult with coaches and teammates to clarify any doubts. Step 2: Choose a Routine Explanation: Work together with your coach and teammates to select an appropriate routine that highlights everyone's strengths while adhering to the competition's guidelines. Consider factors such as skill level, music selection, theme, and audience appeal when making this decision. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Opt for a routine within your team's collective skill range. * Select age-appropriate music and themes. * Make sure the chosen routine aligns with the competition's time limits. Step 3: Create a Training Schedule Well what's the matter Joseph, it's not making music, huh. Goat, Goat, Goat Rationale: This lesson will help children identify /g/, the phoneme represented by G. Students will learn how to recognize this /g/ sound through practice and identifying the sound with a familiar facial gesture, and the sound that a goat makes. Students will gain a confidence of using this sound in their everyday speech along with having learned a new sound developing technique to help them use the same technique to learn new correspondences! Primary paper and pencil Picture of a goat Word cards with words containing the /g/ and words without the /g/: With /g/: green, grass, glue, gum, grow Without /g/: paper, blue, pillow, book Sentence strips with tongue twisters: 1. Gabe got green gumballs when he grew. 2. Gifts are great to give grumpy Gary. 1. Who can tell me what sounds we make when we say, goat? It sounds like /Gggggooooaaat/. What does our mouth and throat look like? (I will tell the students and model it by having them look at my face, "Our face looks like a big circle, making it very round and open.") Goooat, Goooat, Goooat! That's right! This sound is the same whenever we read the short /g/ sound in a word. 2. Now, let's go over the sound that our throat makes when we say that sound. What is the tongue doing? Does the shape of our mouth change? (Let's imitate a goat talking to another goat). 3. Let me show you how to find /g/ in the word gum. I am going to stretch out this word very slowly, so listen for that Goooat sound to appear. /Ggggg/ um. Slower: /Ggggggg/ um. Did you hear it? There it was! I could picture a goat having helped me pronounce that word! 4. Since we figured out that sound in "gum," lets all try some tongue twisters together! The first one is, "Gabe got green gumballs when he grew." Let's all say that together three times as a class. Now, lets slow it down and, emphasize the /g/ sound at the beginning of the words. "Gabe got green gumballs when he grew." (Ggggabe ggggot ggggreen ggggumballs when he ggggrew) That was great! Now, lets do that same exercise again with another tongue twister. (Repeat steps) "Gifts are great to give grumpy Gary." (Ggggifts are ggggreat to ggggive ggggrumpy GGGGary) 5. (I will now hand out primary paper and pencils to everyone.) Now lets practice writing the letter g! There is a difference between the upper case and lower case g's, so we are going to practice writing both of them. Lets start with the uppercase G. (I will model it on the board while I am speaking out loud to the students so that they are two means of learning how to write the letters correctly.) With the uppercase G, you will start at the rooftop, make a curve past the fence and all the way down to the sidewalk. Once you've hit the sidewalk, continue that curve back up to the fence and then make a little straight line going sideways. Well done everyone! Now, lets try the lowercase g. With the lowercase g, you will stay at the fence and below to write this letter. Start at the fence and make a circle to the left, that curves down to the sidewalk and back up the fence. Next, to the right of your circle, at the fence level, make a line that moves downward toward the sidewalk but curves just before it reaches the sidewalk. There you go! You guys are great! 6. Now, I have eight cards here with different words on them. Some of them have the /g/ sound in them and some do not. I am going to call on different students and I want you to read the word aloud and tell me if that sound is or is not in there and explain why or why not. (The list of words I will use are referenced in the materials section.) 7. We are going to reference the book, Eating the Alphabet: Fruits and Vegetables from A to Z, by Lois Ehlert, for further explanation. In this book, each page focuses on a letter and gives examples of different foods starting with that letter. What are some examples of "g" letter foods? We will reference the book for the g letter examples and then each student can pick their favorite food from that page and draw it on a separate sheet of paper. 8. Now, I am going to give you some rhyming words, and I want you to tell me which one has the /g/ sound. For example, go and no. The G tells me to make the gggooat sound, so the right one is, ggggo. Now you try some! Greet or feet? May or gray? Got or hot? Gate or fate? 9. Assessment: Students will be given a worksheet with different objects and words on it. The first five questions of the worksheet, will be to circle the object that contains the /g/ sound. The last five questions will be objects and their names written to the side. The student must match which object goes with which /g/ sounding word. Once upon a time, in a big forest, there was a small bunny named Ben. Ben loved to play with his friends, but one day, he felt hurt in his leg. He didn't know what to do, so he went to ask his friend, the wise owl. The wise owl saw Ben's hurt leg and said, "Oh no, Ben! You need to rest." Ben didn't want to rest, but he knew the wise owl was right. He asked the owl, "How long do I need to rest?" The wise owl gave a little shrug and said, "You need to rest for one whole season." Ben was sad, but he knew he had to listen. He spent the whole season resting and watching his friends play. When the new season came, Ben's leg was all better. He was so happy, and he ran to tell the wise owl, "Thank you for helping me!" From then on, Ben played with his friends and took care of his legs, so he wouldn't get hurt again. On the drain board in the kitchen and a pair of space boots on the mat by the front door. Eating a hot samosa can be a delightful and satisfying experience. However, eating a hot samosa requires some care and attention to avoid burns or an uncomfortable level of spiciness. Here is a step-by-step guide to help you enjoy your hot samosa with ease: **Step 1: Choose Your Samosa** Before you start eating, make sure you have selected a fresh and well-cooked samosa from a reputable source. Look for crispy golden brown skin without any visible signs of burning or undercooking. A good samosa should also have a balanced filling of potatoes, peas, and spices that are evenly distributed throughout. Key tip: Choosing a high-quality samosa ensures maximum flavor and reduces the risk of foodborne illnesses. **Step 2: Allow It to Cool Down** Once you have obtained your samosa, let it cool down for a few minutes before attempting to eat it. This allows the excess oil to drain away and prevents burns from the hot filling. You can place the samosa on a paper towel to absorb any extra grease while it cools. Key guideline: Waiting at least two to three minutes will significantly reduce the temperature of the samosa, making it safer and more comfortable to consume. **Step 3: Find a Comfortable Seating Area** Find a comfortable spot where you can sit and savor your samosa without feeling rushed or distracted. Make sure there's enough space around you to prevent accidental spills or drops. Key tip: Having a drink nearby (such as water, milk, or lassi) can help soothe your mouth if the samosa turns out to be too spicy. **Step 4: Break Apart the Samosa** Gently break apart the samosa along its seams using your fingers or a fork. Be careful not to tear the entire casing open, as this may cause the filling to fall out. The goal is to create small gaps through which you can access the interior contents. Key guideline: By breaking the samosa into smaller pieces, you distribute the heat more evenly across multiple bites and minimize the chance of injury. **Step 5: Remove Excess Skin** Peel back and remove any loose or overly crispy layers of dough from the exterior of the samosa. These parts tend to retain heat longer than the rest of the pastry and can lead to unexpected bursts of discomfort when consumed. Key tip: Discard these excess skins or save them for later as a crunchy snack. **Step 6: Taste Test** Take a small bite of one piece of the broken samosa to test its temperature and spice levels. If the samosa still feels too hot, wait another minute or two before proceeding. Assess whether the spice level is manageable; if it's too intense, consider having that cooling drink mentioned earlier. Key guideline: Always perform a taste test before consuming larger portions of the samosa to ensure optimal comfort and enjoyment. **Step 7: Enjoy Your Samosa** With all precautions taken, feel free to indulge in your delicious hot samosa! Remember to take breaks between bites and sip on your chosen beverage as needed. Key tip: Savor every moment - after all, enjoying food is about both flavor and experience! In this lesson, trigonometric ratios (sine, cosine, and tangent) will be used in real world applications. First we will review the basics: In a right triangle, one angle is 90 and the side across from this angle is called the hypotenuse. The two sides which form the 90 angle are called the legs of the right triangle. We show a right triangle below. The legs are defined as either "opposite" or "adjacent" (next to) the angle We shall call the opposite side "opp," the adjacent side "adj" and the hypotenuse "hyp." Definitions: In the following definitions, sine is called "sin," cosine is called "cos" and tangent is called "tan." The origin of these terms relates to arcs and tangents to a circle. Title: A Comprehensive Tutorial on Utilizing Abuse 3D Notes in Creo Parametric (formerly known as Pro/Engineer) Introduction: Creo Parametric, formerly known as Pro/Engineer, offers an array of features that enable designers and engineers to create robust 3D models efficiently. Among these is the "Abuse 3D Notes" feature, which allows users to add textual information directly onto 3D model faces or bodies. This tutorial will guide you through utilizing this tool effectively while providing valuable insights into its applications and benefits. Step 1: Launching Creo Parametric Start by launching your copy of Creo Parametric on your computer system. Upon successful initialization, proceed to open an existing part file or start creating a new one depending upon your requirements. Step 2: Navigating the Graphics Window Familiarize yourself with the graphics window where all modeling activities take place. Locate the Model Tree pane on the left-hand side; it displays the hierarchical structure of the components within your assembly or parts. On the right side resides the PropertyManager, which changes dynamically based on context. Step 3: Activating the 3D Note Feature To activate the 3D note function, navigate to the 'Annotate' tab located at the top ribbon menu. Within this tab, locate and click on the '3D Note' button found inside the 'Text' dropdown group. Alternatively, type '3dn' in the command line followed by pressing Enter. Key Tip: Remember keyboard shortcuts like '3dn' can significantly speed up your workflow once mastered. Step 4: Placement of the 3D Note Once activated, notice how your cursor transforms into a cross hair symbol indicating readiness for placement. Click on any face or body within your 3D model where you wish to position the 3D note. Make sure to select an appropriate location that does not obstruct visibility or functionality. Step 5: Customizing Text Content After placing the 3D note, a dialog box named '3D Note Definition' appears prompting you to input desired text content. Type in relevant information such as dimensions, tolerances, material callouts, etc., ensuring they adhere to industry standards and conventions. Guideline: Always maintain professionalism when drafting technical communication. Use standard abbreviations and avoid grammatical errors. Step 6: Formatting Options Utilize formatting tools provided within the '3D Note Definition' dialog box to enhance legibility and presentation. These include font selection, size adjustment, color customization, justification settings, leader creation, and more. Best Practice: Consistent styling across documents improves overall visual harmony and facilitates understanding. Step 7: Finalizing the 3D Note Upon completing the text entry and applying necessary formatting, finalize the process by clicking 'OK'. Observe how the entered data now becomes visible on the selected face/body of your 3D model as per specified parameters. Step 8: Editing Existing 3D Notes Should modifications be required post initial creation, simply double-click the respective 3D note causing the '3D Note Definition' dialog box to reappear. Edit the desired fields, format accordingly, then confirm changes via 'OK'. Remember, clear and concise communication forms the backbone of effective engineering design practices. By incorporating 3D notes judiciously, we enrich our designs with essential metadata enhancing comprehension and usability downstream. Happy designing! And then you can teach them all the letters. [stands up black base, moves yellow wand towards it and then rapidly away so that it falls over on the table] Title: How to Dive and Pass a Volleyball like a Pro Volleyball is an exciting and fast-paced sport that requires quick reflexes, agility, and excellent teamwork. One essential skill every volleyball player should master is diving and passing. This move enables you to reach balls hit far from your position and maintain possession while setting up offensive plays. Here's our comprehensive guide to help you dive and pass effectively: ### Step 1: Prepare yourself mentally Anticipate that there will be instances when you need to leave your designated area to save or pass the ball. Understand that this action might put you at risk of minor injuries such as scraped skin or bruises. Be prepared to take these risks and always prioritize safety by knowing your limits and practicing proper techniques. ### Step 2: Perfect your platform stance A solid base is crucial before attempting any dive. Position your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and weight evenly distributed between both legs. Your body should form a tripod with two points of contact (both feet) on the ground and one point of contact (your dominant hand) above the waist level holding the ball. Keep your non-dominant arm extended towards the target to improve balance and stability. Maintain a straight back and avoid hunching over. Key tip: Regular practice of the platform stance ensures correct muscle memory during actual games. ### Step 3: Master the approach When preparing to dive, start by taking short strides toward the direction where the ball is headed. As you stride forward, bend your leading knee and extend your arms outward simultaneously. Swing your opposite arm backward for momentum and balance. Remember to keep your eyes focused on the ball throughout the process. Guideline: The goal here is to create maximum speed and power without sacrificing control and accuracy. ### Step 4: Initiate the dive As soon as you determine the trajectory of the incoming ball, commit fully to the dive. Lean your torso forward, keeping your hips high and driving your lead foot into the court. Extend your arms further if necessary to reach the ball. At this stage, ensure that your shoulders are aligned with your hips, maintaining a streamlined posture. Key tip: It's important to initiate the dive early enough so you can cover sufficient distance but late enough to allow adjustments based on last-minute changes in the ball's path. ### Step 5: Make contact with the ball Once airborne, tuck your chin close to your chest, spotting the ball until impact. Aim to make contact near your forehead or crown using your fingertips. Apply slight pressure downwards to direct the ball towards your intended target - usually your setter or another teammate positioned strategically. Guideline: Focus on controlling the ball rather than catching it. Also, remember to cushion the impact by bending your elbows slightly upon contact. ### Step 6: Land safely Land on your side or hip whenever possible to reduce shock absorption through your joints. Bend your landing leg at the knee and roll onto your buttocks if needed. Immediately get back on your feet ready for the next play. Key tip: Never land on your wrists, hands, or outstretched fingers, as doing so could result in severe injury. Remember, consistent practice is vital to becoming proficient at diving and passing in volleyball. Always warm-up properly before engaging in strenuous activities and listen to your body signs to prevent burnout or serious harm. With patience, determination, and persistence, you'll soon find yourself executing impressive dives and passes effortlessly! oh, I thought you said I'll be... Title: How to Dress Up in a Pencil Skirt with Color Blocking A pencil skirt is a versatile wardrobe staple that can be dressed up or down for various occasions. Adding color blocking to your outfit can create visual interest and make you stand out. This tutorial will guide you through dressing up in a pencil skirt with color blocking, providing in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines to help you achieve a stylish look. **Step 1: Choose Your Pencil Skirt** First, select a well-fitted pencil skirt that flatters your body shape. The length should ideally fall just above or below the knee. A neutral color like black, navy, or gray provides a great base for color blocking, as they are easy to pair with bright colors. *Key tip:* Avoid skirts that are too tight or too loose; ensure comfort and mobility while maintaining an elegant silhouette. **Step 2: Select Bold Colors for Top & Accessories** Choose two bold, complementary colors for your top and accessories. For example, if your skirt is black, consider pairing it with a vibrant yellow blouse and red heels. Refer to a color wheel to find complementary colors (colors opposite each other) or analogous colors (colors next to each other). *Guideline:* Stick to two main colors plus one metallic accent (e.g., gold jewelry) to prevent overwhelming the outfit. **Step 3: Find a Well-Fitting Blouse** Select a blouse that fits well and suits the occasion. It could have a structured fit for formal events or a flowing fabric for casual settings. Make sure the neckline compliments your figure - crewnecks elongate the torso, scoop necks widen the chest area, and V-necks lengthen the upper body. *Key tip:* If choosing a fitted blouse, ensure there's enough room for movement without restricting breathing or causing discomfort. **Step 4: Pick Out Complementary Shoes** Your shoe choice depends on the formality of the event. Heels add elegance and height but may not always be suitable. Flats or loafers work fine for more laid-back environments. Regardless of type, opt for shoes in one of your chosen bold colors. *Guideline:* Match your shoe color to either your top or bag, whichever stands out more prominently than the other. **Step 5: Accessorize Thoughtfully** Add finishing touches such as earrings, bracelets, watches, or scarves in your second bold color or a metallic hue. Keep it simple yet impactful - avoid overloading on accessories. *Key tip:* Consider wearing a belt that matches your shoes to tie together the entire ensemble. **Step 6: Apply Minimalistic Makeup** Go for natural-looking makeup focusing on enhancing your features rather than distracting from them. Use your bold colors subtly by incorporating them into lipstick, eyeshadow, or nail polish. *Guideline:* Balance intensity between facial features; if going heavy on lips, keep eye makeup minimal and vice versa. **Step 7: Confidence Check!** Lastly, remember confidence is key when pulling off any outfit. Stand tall, smile, and own the space around you. With these steps followed correctly, you're ready to turn heads at whatever function you attend! Watch this film carefully. Some people make pictures that do not look flat, by taking two regular flat pictures at the same time. People have to move their eyes in a special way or use a stereoscope so they can see both pictures together. When they look at the pictures using their binocular vision correctly, the picture does not look flat. This is called '3D' or 'three dimensional'. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/07/5837AK.cha = = = The hall was cold and in semi-darkness, but through the half-opened door of his mother's chamber came a gush of firelight warm and bright. Two plates can move towards each other ("convergent" plate edges). This can form islands (such as Japan), volcanoes, and high mountain ranges (such as the Andes and Himalayas). Two plates can move away from each other ("divergent" plate edges). This gives the warm liquid rock inside the earth a place to come out. This makes special mountain ranges below the sea or large low lands like Africa's Great Rift Valley. Plates are able to move beside each other as well ("transform" plate edges, such as the San Andreas Fault). This makes their edges crush against each other and makes many shocks as they move. [puts the heart back into the mitten] [SS has begun to knock on the outside door] Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's talk about two interesting ideas in the world of modules - a concept that builds upon the idea of groups, which you may have learned about in earlier grades. First, imagine you have a big box (which we will call our module) full of different colored marbles (our elements). Now, suppose someone gives you a magic chute (a special kind of function or map) that takes marbles out of the box and rearranges them before placing them back into the same box. The challenge is to figure out when you can get ALL the original marbles back using just this magic chute. Here's where being "Noetherian" comes in handy! A Noetherian box is one that follows a specific rule: there cannot be infinitely many sub-boxes containing only newer marbles. In other words, once a marble appears in some position within the larger box, it must also appear in every subsequent sub-box. Now, let me tell you something fascinating: if your magical chute is both surjective (meaning it covers all possible outcomes in the box) and your box is Noetherian, then you can indeed recover all your original marbles through the chute! That's because any time a new pattern shows up among the marbles, it would eventually repeat due to the finite nature of Noetherian boxes. And since our chute is surjective, every single marble must return after enough iterations. Isn't that cool? Next, let's think about another property called "semi-simplicity." When a module is semi-simple, it means that it breaks down easily into smaller pieces called simple modules. These little guys don't have any further nontrivial decomposition-they're as basic as building blocks can get! Now, consider a situation where a semisimple module ALSO happens to be Noetherian. Surprisingly, under those conditions, not only does it decompose nicely into simple parts, but it remains well-behaved when arranged differently; specifically, its reverse arrangement still respects the rules of being Noetherian. As a result, it turns out that such a module is Artinian too! An Artinian module shares similarities with our Noetherian friend but focuses more on having finitely many nested sub-boxes rather than their contents obeying certain patterns. So remember, next time you find yourself playing around with colorful marbles or organizing things neatly, think about whether they share properties like Noetherian and Semi-simple ones. Who knows? Maybe you'll discover new exciting ways to play or understand the world better! See ya make it down 'n' around. Mass is an "inertial property". It measures the tendency of an object to stay at a given speed when no force acts on it. Title: Celebrating Grandparents' Day When Your Grandparents Are No Longer Alive Grandparents' Day is a special occasion to show appreciation and love towards our grandparents. However, if your grandparents are no longer alive, you might feel like there isn't much to celebrate. But that doesn't have to be the case! Here's a guide on how to commemorate this day and honor their memory in meaningful ways. ### Step 1: Acknowledge Your Feelings Before anything else, acknowledge your feelings about your grandparents' passing. It's okay to feel sad or miss them during this time. Recognizing these emotions will help you process them better and make your celebration more therapeutic. ### Step 2: Educate Yourself About Grandparents' Day Understanding the significance of Grandparents' Day can provide context and motivation for your tribute. This national holiday was established in the United States in 1978 to honor grandparents, give strength and support to our families, and encourage respect between generations. ### Step 3: Reflect On Their Lives Spend some time reflecting on what made your grandparents unique. Think about their stories, values, hobbies, careers, traditions, or even quirks that made them who they were. Writing down these memories can be helpful. ### Step 4: Create a Memorial Space Designate an area in your home where you can display photos, mementos, letters, or other items that remind you of your grandparents. This could be a shelf, table, or wall space. Having this visual reminder allows you to connect with them throughout the year, not just on Grandparents' Day. ### Step 5: Cook One Of Their Favorite Meals Food has a way of bringing back fond memories. Prepare one of your grandparent's favorite dishes as a way of honoring them. If possible, share this meal with family members or friends who also knew your grandparents. Sharing stories while enjoying the food enhances the experience. ### Step 6: Donate To a Charitable Cause They Supported If your grandparents had a particular charity or cause they cared deeply about, consider making a donation in their name. Not only does this keep their legacy alive, but it also positively impacts others. ### Step 7: Watch Home Movies Or Listen To Audio Recordings Revisiting old home movies or listening to audio recordings of your grandparents can bring comfort and joy. These moments offer insight into their personalities and allow you to hear their voices again. ### Step 8: Share Stories With Younger Generations Preserve your grandparents' legacy by sharing stories about them with younger relatives or family friends. By doing so, you pass along their wisdom and experiences to future generations. ### Step 9: Visit Their Grave Site or Memorial Pay respects at your grandparents' burial place or memorial site. Bring flowers, light candles, say prayers, or simply spend quiet time remembering them. ### Step 10: Plant Something In Their Memory Planting a tree, flower, or shrub in your garden or local park is a living tribute to your grandparents. As it grows, it serves as a symbol of remembrance and continuity. Remember, celebrating Grandparents' Day without your grandparents present is not about forgetting; rather, it's about cherishing their lives and keeping their spirit alive through various acts of remembrance. Title: Convert an Image to HTML Code Introduction: In this extensive guide, you'll learn how to convert an image into HTML code using various techniques. This skill is particularly useful when designing emails or websites that require specific imagery without hosting external files. By converting your image into base64 encoding and embedding it within an `<img>` tag, you can display images directly through HTML. Let's dive into the process! **Step 1:** Selecting the Right Tool To convert an image to HTML, first choose an appropriate online tool such as **Base64 Images**, **Data URI Scheme Generator**, or **CSS Drive**. These tools simplify the conversion process by generating base64 encoded data URIs from inputted images. Manually coding base64 yourself isn't recommended due to its complexity and susceptibility to errors. Key Tips: - Always ensure selected tools are reliable and secure since they handle file uploads. - Opt for well-known and established tools with positive user reviews. **Step 2:** Preparing Your Image Before uploading, optimize your image (PNG, JPEG, GIF) for web usage via resizing, cropping, and compression. Aim for under 50 KB if possible; smaller sizes reduce page load times and improve overall performance. Use editing software like Adobe Photoshop, GIMP, or free online editors such as Pixlr, Canva, etc., depending upon your requirement and comfort level. Guidelines: - Maintain aspect ratio while resizing to avoid distorted images. - Optimize for target devices - mobile, tablet, desktop - based on project needs. - Save final image at 72 dpi (dots per inch), suitable for screen displays. **Step 3:** Converting the Image Upload your prepared image to the chosen tool and initiate the conversion process. The generator will create a unique string of characters representing the binary data of your image, known as a _base64 encoded data URI_. Copy this string for later use in creating the HTML code. Key Points: - Base64 encoding increases file size slightly compared to original format. - Some older browsers may have limited support for larger base64 strings. **Step 4:** Coding the Data URI into HTML Within the `<body>` section of your HTML document, insert an `<img>` tag containing the base64 encoded data URI copied earlier. Format the `src` attribute accordingly: ```html <img src="data:image/png;base6 ah, ah this one's yours that one's yours they're both yours. Sarah would you give me a banana? ATE Not AT order to become skilled readers, students must develop skills that will them decode words successfully. Part of learning to decode is being able to distinguish between long and short vowels. With vowels, students must each vowel makes more than one sound. This lesson will review the correspondence a = /a/ and introduce /A/ using direct instruction, decodable books, and pseudo-words. 1. First, review a = /a/, then introduce a_e = /A/. Say: "Today, we are going to talk about the letter a. We have learned that a says /a/ in words, but the letter a can say another sound. When we see the letter a, then a consonant, then the letter e, the letter a says its name /A/. Let's look at the word made. (Write made on the dry erase board.) We can see that the letter a is followed by a consonant (d), and then has the letter e on the end. Do we hear the letter e said at the end of the word? /m/ /A/ /d/ mad! No, we don't hear the e so it's silent. That e just lets you know to say /A/ instead of /a/. Let's take another look at the word made. If we take off the e, what word do we have? (Erase the e). Mad, good job! Let's try another word. (Write can on the board). What word is this? /c/ /a/ /n/ can. Now if I add an e to the end, that changes how we say the word. (Add the e on the board). Now it says /c/ /A/ /n/ cane! Now you will try." (Repeat sequence with the words hat/hate and glad/glade. 2. Teacher explains: "We will practice finding when A says its name. I have a tongue twister for us to say to find that /A/ sound. I will say it first, then we will repeat it together. "Dave the snake waved to Kate." Let's say it together, "Dave the snake waved to Kate." Great job! Now, we are going to stretch that /A/ sound out nice and long! I will say it, then we will say it together. "Daaaave the snaaaake waaaaved to Kaaaate." Together: "Daaaave the snaaaake waaaaved to Kaaaate." Awesome!" 3. The next step is to practice a_e = /A/ using the Elkonin letterboxes. The students will have already used this method to learn various other correspondences prior to this lesson. The lesson consists of review words with short vowel sounds, and uses the new correspondence. The words are broken down by the number of phonemes, from 2 to 5 phoneme words. The teacher will use a set of an Elkonin box and letters to model a word for the students. Teacher explains: "We are going to use our letterboxes to help us spell some words. Today, we have learned that the a_e = /A/. That e is silent, so it will not go in a box. The e will sit directly on the outside of the box so we know to say /A/ instead of /a/. Let me show you how I would spell the word scrape. The first sound that I hear is the /s/, so I am going to put the letter s in my first box. Now this word has a lot of sounds at the beginning, so I am going to stretch it out really slow to hear them all. Ssss-cccc-rrrr-AAAA-pppp. I have my s in the box and the next sound I heard was /c/. I'm going to put my c in the next box. S-c-rrrr-AAAA-pppp. There's the r, so I'll put that down next. Scr-AAAA-ppp. I hear my long A! What does that mean? I will place the letter a in the fourth box and because it says it's name, I have to put that e on the end. I'm still missing a sound, so I'll say the word one last time. ScraA-pppp. P, that's what I'm missing. (Teacher hands out letterbox bags). Students, please get out your letterboxes and letters. I am going to give you some words to spell. The following words will be used for the LBL. They include review vowels as well as the new correspondence. It is helpful to say a sentence after each word so students understand the context. 2 phoneme words: ate 3 phoneme words: puck, made, cab, date 4 phoneme words: plane, frog, drake, spat 5 phoneme words: scrape Next, write the words the students spelled on the board. Call on one student at a time to read the selected words. Depending on the class size, students can read one, two, or all of the words. 4. Pass out individual copies of James and the Good Day. Say: "Now students, we are going to read a great book called James and the Good Day. You will get into pairs and read out loud to one another. If you have a problem, raise your hand and I will help you. This story is about a little boy named James that wakes up on a Saturday and decides to play. He really wants to play with his tug, so he makes a lake in his bathtub. James gets tired of waiting so he leaves the room to play with his other toys. But soon, the lake overflows onto the floor! What is James going to do? Will he get in trouble with his Mom? Let's read to find out!" 5. After reading, students will take out a sheet of primary paper and a pencil. Teacher instructs: "Friends, it's time to write a message! I want you to write me a message about your favorite thing to do on a Saturday. If you don't know how to spell a word, use your invented spelling to do the best you can." 6. Pass out a worksheet to each student with the words HAT, GAP, CAN, AT, SAM, and MAT printed on it. Students should read the words altogether. Teacher will then instruct students to rewrite the word with an e on the end. The students will then read the new words. This activity reinforces a_e = /A/. assessment, the teacher will call students up to the desk individually. Each student will be given a set of index cards with printed on them. Students will then read the words to check for understanding of a = /a/ and a_e = /A/. Example words could be: KAD, PLAME, MAG, HADE, CHAM, and PAKE. James and the Good Day. " " A lift the flap book by Karen Katz." Lily and Anna were best friends. They liked to play with dolls and dress up. One day, they found a pretty box under Lily's bed. It had a lock and a key. "What is in the box?" Anna asked. "I don't know. It is a secret," Lily said. She took the key and opened the box. Inside, there were many shiny things. There were rings, necklaces, bracelets, and earrings. "Wow, they are so pretty!" Anna said. She reached for a ring. "Stop! Don't touch them!" Lily said. She pulled the box away from Anna. "They are mine! They are from my grandma. She gave them to me before she went away. She said they are very special and I have to keep them safe." "But I just want to look at them. Please, can I try one on?" Anna said. She felt sad and angry. She thought Lily was being mean and selfish. "No, you can't! They are not for you. They are only for me. Go away!" Lily said. She closed the box and put it back under the bed. She turned her back to Anna. Anna felt tears in her eyes. She did not want to play with Lily anymore. She got up and ran out of the room. She did not say goodbye. Lily heard Anna leave. She felt lonely and sorry. She did not mean to hurt Anna. She loved her friend. She wanted to share her pretty things with her. But she was afraid to lose them. She remembered how her grandma hugged her and told her to take good care of them. She missed her grandma very much. She got the box out again and opened it. She looked at the shiny things. They were pretty, but they did not make her happy. She wished Anna was there with her. She wanted to say sorry and give her a hug. She wanted to tell her the secret of the box. She wanted to play with her again. She took the key and ran after Anna. She hoped she would find her and make up with her. She hoped Anna would still be her friend. She hoped they could seat together and look at the pretty things and smile. d'Aulnoy. One day, however, a sea captain came and asked Halvor if he hadn't a fancy to come with him and go to sea, and behold foreign lands. The resolution of pit eyes can be improved a lot by adding a material to make a lens. This will reduce the radius of the blurring, and increase the resolution that can be achieved. The most basic form can still be seen in some gastropods and annelids. These eyes have a lens of one refractive index. It is possible to get a better image with materials that have a high refractive index which decreases towards the edges. The divisions of Greece are called 'Peripheries'. As of January 2011, there are 13 peripheries in Greece. Peripheries are subdivided into 'peripheral units', and previously they were known as 'prefecture', but prefectures were abolished in 2011. The most populated peripheries in Greece are Attica, where the capital city of Greece, Athens, is, and Central Macedonia, where Greece's second-largest city, Thessaloniki, is. By the early 19th century the sound that the piano made was big enough to fill large concert halls. Smaller pianos were made for use in people's homes. At first these included square pianos and giraffe pianos, later on the upright pianos became popular for home use. Pianos are not often used in orchestras (if they are, they are part of the percussion section). They may, however, be used for piano concertos (pieces for solo pianist accompanied by orchestra). There is a vast amount of music written for piano solo. What do you put on over your pajamas? Is that what you get in your lunch? For a child to become a fluent reader, he/she must begin by understanding the fact that letters represent phonemes. Phonemes are defined as vocal gestures that they hear. They must also express an understanding that spelling maps out the sequence of phonemes in spoken words. We can see their development of reading, as the children begin to recognize phonemes. The following lesson will assist students in discovering the a=/a/ correspondence. Students will also learn to recognize /a/ in - Picture of a baby crying (one per studnent) - Worksheet with the following pictures on it: nap, cat, apple, fish, house, ant, circle, fat, ham, appricots, book, alligator, bag, - A Cat Nap, Carson, CA: Educational Insights, 1990. - primary paper (per student) - pencils (per student) 1.Introduction of the lesson may begin my saying: "What sound does a baby make when it is crying?" While asking this, hold up a picture of a baby crying. The student should respond with /a/. If they make a different sound or make no sound at all then model it for them and have them repeat, /a/. While modeling, hold hands up to your eyes as if you are rubbing your tears and have the student repeat. (If you prefer or find it better for the student, you could just have the child hold up the picture of the baby crying). 2.Tell the students: "A baby is not the only thing that makes that sound, but we can find that sound in many words." 3.Tell the students: "I am going to read several words to you and I want you to pay close attention. Each time you hear the /a/ sound, I want you to rub your eyes, like a baby crying (or hold up the baby card) which ever you and the student decided was best. It may vary from child to child. I'll do it with you this time." Use some words as the following (mix the words up and don't have the a = /a/ correspondence in all of A few example words include: 4."I am now going to read you a funny sentence. Please pay close attention because I want you to rub your eyes like a baby (or hold up picture of baby crying) each time you hear /a/, but I'm not going to do it with you this time." Watch students as you read the sentence and if they seem to be struggling or getting it incorrect, let them finish the sentence, but go back and have them repeat it, and the second time, help them out, modeling it while they do the activity. Then go back a third time and see if they can do it solo. Keep an eye on the students to check for understanding and participation. the Ant had apples and apricots" 5."This time when we read the sentence we will stretch out the /a/ sound. Open your mouth wide like an aaaligator to stretch the sounds out. It might sound funny but it will be fun. Lets do it the Aaaant haaad aaapples aaand aaapricots" 6.Take out a piece of paper and a pencil. Use the letter a to write /a/. Start at the rooftop, go down the slide to the sidewalk and cross at the fence. I will walk around to see everyone's a. Make five more. 7. Now read A Cat Nap to the students. Tell the students: "Each time you hear /a/ I want you to put up your hands like your crying (or hold up the baby crying card)." Teacher should be conscious to read slowly and clearly, enunciating well, so the students can keep up as he/she reads. 8. To assess the children, hand each of them a page that has pictures on it (worksheet). Students will say the words out loud and circle the pictures that have the /a/ sound in them. Some example words to use (Be sure the pictures are distinct, so no confusion will cause the student to miss a question): A Cat Nap, Carson, CA: Educational Insights, 1990. "Cry Baby". Rational: This lesson will help children identify /s/, the phoneme that /s/ is represented by S. Students will learn to recognize /s/ in spoken words, how to write an upper case S and lower case s, and how to distinguish between words that have /s/ and words that do not have /s/. Materials: Primary paper and pencils; Chart with "Seth Snake slithers sneakily down the sizzling hot sidewalk"; card with the letter S; Scoot, by Cathryn Falwell 1. Say: "We use the letters of the alphabet to write and say words every day. It is very important that we learn the sounds that go with each letter of the alphabet so we can use them correctly. Today we are going to be working on the sound /s/. We spell /s/ using the letter S." 2. Say: "Let's make the /s/ sound. Can you think of an animal that makes the /s/ sound? That's right. . . a snake makes the /s/ sound. Every time we hear /s/ we are going to pretend that we are snakes and we are going to make our hands slither like snakes." (Demonstrate the motion.) 3. Say: "Now we are going to make the /s/ sound and we are going to pay very close attention to what our mouth and tongue do when we say /s/. (Say /s/.) When we say this our teeth should be barely open and our tongue should be lightly touching the back of your teeth and front of your mouth. Let's say it again and make sure our mouth and tongue are doing the right thing." 4. Say: "Now we are going to practice /s/ with a tongue tickler. Don't forget to use your slithering snake hands!" (Point to each word as student is saying it.) "'Seth Snake slithers sneakily down the sizzling hot sidewalk.' Good job with all those /s/'s! Now let's say it again, and this time, stretch the /s/ at the beginning of each word. 'SSSSeth SSSSnake SSSSlithers SSSSneakily down the SSSSizzling hot SSSSidewalk.' Wonderful! Now we are going to try it again, but this time we are going to break the /s/ off of each word: '/S/ eth /S/ nake /S/ lithers /S/ neakily down the /S/ izzling hot /S/ idewalk.' Fantastic!" 5. Say: "Now I am going to show you how to find the /s/ in words. I am going to use the word snail. Ssss-n-ai-l. Do you hear the /s/ in ssss-n-ai-l. In the word snail, the /s/ is at the beginning, but sometimes it can be in the middle or at the end. Okay, now I am going to say some other words, and I want you to use your snake hands when you hear /s/." (house, mask) 6. Say: "Since we know what /s/ sounds like and we can recognize it in words, now we need to practice writing it. We use the letter S to write /s/." Show them the card with the letter S. "The letter S even looks like a snake. We are going to start by writing the lower case s. Watch me write s and then you can try. We start just below the fence and make a c, and then we curve down to the side walk. I want you to write one s. When you have finished writing one s raise your hand and I will come by to make sure it is written correctly. After I check your s I want you to write five more s's for practice." (After everyone has had enough practice continue with the lesson.) "Now we are going to practice the upper case S. It looks just like the lower case s except it is bigger. We start just below the rooftop and make our c that stops at the fence, and then we curve down to the side walk. When you have written one S raise your hand so that I can come check it. After I check your S you can write five more to practice." 7. Say: "Now I am going to say different words and I want you to tell me which one you hear the /s/ in." (Sand or water, float or sink, plate or base). 8. Say: "We are going to read a story now. We will be reading Scoot, by Cathryn Falwell. This book is about lots of animals in the woods, and they like to play and jump around in the woods, except for these six turtles. They like to sit, and not move, but strong winds come upon the turtles. What do you think will happen to the six silent turtles? Let's read it to find out! While I am reading the story I want you to listen for the /s/. . . don't forget to use your snake arms when you hear /s/!" Assessment: I will give students a worksheet with different pictures. I will tell students to circle the picture if it has the /s/ sound in it. Then I will have them write an upper case S and a lower case s. http://www.auburn.edu/academic/education/reading_genie/doorways/colvinel.htm (Slithering, Sneaky Snake Says "SSSsssss" by Janie Colvin) http://www.auburn.edu/academic/education/reading_genie/doorways/burrcel.htm (Sassy Sally Snake by Caroline Burr) http://www.auburn.edu/academic/education/reading_genie/doorways/jacksonhel.htm (Slithering Snakes by Hannah Jackson) Falwell, Cathryn. If you want text to appear on multiple lines in a cell, you can format the cell so that the text wraps automatically, or you can enter a manual line break. What do you want to do? Wrap text automatically - In a worksheet, select the cells that you want to format. - On the Home tab, in the Alignment group, click Wrap Text . - Data in the cell wraps to fit the column width. When you change the column width, data wrapping adjusts automatically. - If all wrapped text is not visible, it may be because the row is set to a specific height or that the text is in a range of cells that has been merged. To make all wrapped text visibly, do the following to manually adjust the row height: - Select the cell or range for which you want to adjust the row height. - On the Home tab, in the Cells group, click Format. - Under Cell Size , do one of the following: - To automatically adjust the row height, click AutoFit Row Height. - To specify a row height, click Row Height, and then type the row height that you want in the Row height box. Tip You can also drag the bottom border of the row to the height that shows all wrapped text. Top of Page Enter a line break You can start a new line of text at any specific point in a cell. - Double-click the cell in which you want to enter a line break. Keyboard shortcut You can also select the cell, and then press F2. - In the cell, click the location where you want to break the line, and then press ALT+ENTER. The National Tree of Belize is the Mahogany Tree ("Swietenia macrophylla"), one of the magnificent giants of the Belize rain forest. It rises straight and tall to over a hundred feet. In the early months of the year, when the leaves fall and new red-brown growth appears, the tree can be seen from a great distance. The tree puts out a many small whitish flowers. The flowers blossom into dark fruits, which are pear-shaped capsules about six inches long. The mahogany tree matures in 60 to 80 years. Title: How to Achieve Absolute Positioning with CSS Introduction: In web development, positioning elements accurately is crucial for creating visually appealing and functional websites. One popular method for achieving precise layouts is using absolute positioning with CSS. This technique allows you to place an element exactly where you want it on the page, relative to its nearest positioned ancestor or the body element if no such ancestor exists. This tutorial will guide you through the process of implementing absolute positioning with clear explanations and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Understand the Box Model Before diving into absolute positioning, familiarize yourself with the box model concept in CSS. Each HTML element has content, padding, borders, and margins that together form a rectangular box. When setting widths and heights, these properties relate to the content area only unless specified otherwise (e.g., `box-sizing: border-box;`). Keep this in mind when calculating dimensions and positions. Step 2: Create a Container Element To start using absolute positioning, first create a container element within which your absolutely positioned element(s) will reside. Set the container's position property to either 'relative', 'absolute', or 'fixed'. Typically, 'relative' is used so that the positioned child elements are based on this container instead of the document body. Example: ```css <div class="container"> <!-- Your absolutely positioned elements go here --> </div> ``` ```css .container { position: relative; /* Or absolute / fixed */ } ``` Key Tip: Remember that the type of positioning applied to the parent container affects how absolute positioning behaves for its children. If no positioned ancestors exist, absolute positioning defaults to being relative to the body element. Step 3: Define the Positioned Child Element Now, identify the specific element(s) you wish to position absolutely and set their position property to 'absolute'. You can then utilize top, right, bottom, and left properties to define exact coordinates. Note that percentages refer to the distance from the corresponding edge of the nearest positioned ancestor or the body element if none exists. Example: ```html <div class="container"> <div class="absolutely-positioned"></div> </div> ``` ```css .absolutely-positioned { position: absolute; top: 50px; left: 75px; } ``` Step 4: Consider Overflow Behavior When working with absolute positioning, be aware that overflow settings may affect visibility of your positioned elements. For example, if a containing block has `overflow: hidden`, any part of an absolutely positioned child extending beyond its boundaries could become invisible. Always double check your intended behavior and adjust accordingly. Step 5: Account for Responsive Design Keep responsiveness in mind while employing absolute positioning techniques. Using percentage values rather than fixed units can help maintain proper placement as screen sizes change. Additionally, consider utilizing media queries to make additional adjustments at different breakpoints. Best Practice Guidelines: * Avoid relying solely on absolute positioning for overall layout structure due to potential accessibility issues and difficulty maintaining consistency across various viewport sizes. Instead, reserve it primarily for smaller components like modals or tooltips. * Test your website thoroughly after applying absolute positioning to ensure correct rendering and functionality across multiple devices and browsers. * Make sure there's sufficient contrast between text color and background color for readability purposes when placing overlay divs with absolute positioning. Ye'h we're very uh interested in our belly. view a plan Here students can practice their foreign language vocabulary while playing baseball 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12 Title - Vamos a jugar B isbol! By - Loly Primary Subject - Language Arts Grade Level - 7-12 - The object of this lesson is for students to practice vocabulary, verb conjugations and sentence formation in an oral and aural situation. - Note cards for the vocabulary. English on one side, Spanish on the other. - Note cards for each one of the pronouns. - Chalkboard/whiteboard; chalk; whiteboard markers - Divide the class into two teams. Call them Equipo Uno y Equipo Dos, or whatever you'd like in the target language that will help them learn more words. Primer Equipo, Segundo Equipo etc. - Have the team write up a batter line-up. - On the board, draw a baseball diamond. Using the target vocabulary, label home plate, first base, second base, and third base. Home plate is at the bottom, and the bases go from right to left. - The runner can be depicted with a mark or a magnet that can be moved around the bases. If you use a magnet, you want two distinct looking magnets for each team. - The teacher is the pitcher. - Students cannot use their vocabulary list. - There cannot be more than one runner on the field. - Each batter gets the chance to advance the runner one base. - You will pitch a word in English to the batter. - The batter has to translate quickly. - If they do not know the word within 5 seconds, they don't know the word and they are out. - If they say the word but it's not pronounced correctly, let them know that the word is correct but the pronunciation is off. They have 3 chances, [3 strikes] to pronounce it correctly, otherwise they are out. - If they know the word, and they pronounce it correctly, then they get to a base. - They can steal a base, but first they must state that they want to steal a base. Then they can do one of the following things: - Spell it aloud correctly. - Spell it on paper correctly. - If it's a verb, they can choose a pronoun and conjugate the verb. It must be pronounced correctly. If they are unable to do this, they stay on the same base, if they get it correct, they can steal one base. - They can get a homerun if they can use the word/verb in a sentence that is grammatically correct and complete. Otherwise they stay on the same base. - If the team is able to get their runner all the way home, this earns them a point. If they have a runner on the field and they get three outs, well then there are no points. - If you pitch a word, and the first batter doesn't know it, then it goes to the next batter. Remember they are not allowed to disclose the answer to each other, give hints to each other, etc., otherwise it's an out! If they do, then the batter whose turn it was loses his turn. - Once the team makes three outs, the other team goes to bat, and the same word gets pitched to them. This is where the list comes in handy. The students cannot position themselves according to who knows the answer. Team two will now have the opportunity to make runs. - If the opposing team doesn't know the word either and they get 3 outs right away, then the same word goes back to the other team to the batter who was next. This again is where the list comes in handy. - If either team is unable to get the word, and all the batters have gone up, then the word gets shown to the class and you can have the students write it down 10 times along with the translation or not. - If the game has been explained to the class, you can quickly set up for it and play it the last 20 minutes or half hour of a class. If it's the first time you play it, then you will need at least 15 minutes to explain the rules and get set up. - You can alternate with Bingo and B isbol to study vocabulary. [last 16 lines of transcript are ADS.] Title: How to Cite a Textbook in MLA Format Introduction: In academic writing, citing your sources is crucial to give credit to the original authors and to allow your readers to access the resources you used. The Modern Language Association (MLA) format is commonly used in humanities disciplines like literature, philosophy, and languages. This tutorial will guide you through the process of citing a textbook using MLA 8th edition. Step 1: Identify the Core Elements To create an accurate citation, locate and identify the core elements of the source. These include: - Author(s) or editor(s) - Title of the book and subtitle (if any), including edition statements - Publisher name and location - Publication date - Medium of publication (eBook, Print, etc.) Step 2: Start with the Author's Name The author's last name should come first, followed by their first name, and then middle initial if applicable. Use commas to separate each element and end with a period. For example: Doyle, Arthur Conan. A Study in Scarlet. Key tip: If there are two authors, list them alphabetically based on their last names. Use 'and' instead of an ampersand (&). For three or more authors, only mention the first author followed by 'et al.' Step 3: Add the Book Title and Subtitle Write out the full title and subtitle (if present) in italics. Capitalize all major words within both titles and the subtitle. End with a period after the closing parenthesis. For instance: Doyle, Arthur Conan. A Study in Scarlet. Oxford UP, 2017. Step 4: Specify the Edition Statement If this isn't the first edition of the textbook, indicate so by adding the edition statement right after the title. It usually includes the word 'edition', abbreviated as 'ed.', along with the number of the edition. Follow it up with another period. Here's an illustrative example: Smith, John. Introduction to Psychology. 5th ed., McGraw-Hill Education, 2021. Step 5: Insert the Publisher Information After specifying the publication date, add the publisher information without using articles ('a', 'an', 'the'). Separate different parts of the publisher's name with spaces but no punctuation marks except when necessary (like in acronyms). Conclude this section with a comma. Check this sample entry: Johnson, Mark. Literary Theory: A Very Short Introduction. OUP, 2016. Step 6: State the Medium of Publication Since we are focusing on print textbooks here, simply write 'Print'. However, if it were an eBook, you would need to provide additional details such as URL or DOI. Finish off the citation with a period. Klein, Naomi. No Logo: Taking Aim at the Brand Bullies. 2nd ed., Picador, 2009. Print. Conclusion: Practice makes perfect! Keep practicing these steps until they become second nature. Remember that consistency is key when creating citations; adhere strictly to MLA rules throughout your paper. By correctly crediting your sources, you demonstrate respect for intellectual property rights and enhance your credibility as a scholar. Others are thermoplastic and can be softened by heating them. Chapter X TRIBULATION Mr Harding was a sadder man than he had ever yet been when he returned to his own house. He had been wretched enough on that well-remembered morning when he was forced to expose before his son-in-law the publisher's account for ushering into the world his dear book of sacred music: when after making such payments as he could do unassisted, he found that he was a debtor of more than three hundred pounds; but his sufferings then were as nothing to his present misery;--then he had done wrong, and he knew it, and was able to resolve that he would not sin in like manner again; but now he could make no resolution, and comfort himself by no promises of firmness. He had been forced to think that his lot had placed him in a false position, and he was about to maintain that position against the opinion of the world and against his own convictions. Note that the masses of the planets are lower bounds only. If a planet is detected by the spectral line displacement method referred to above, no information is gained about the inclination of the planet's plane of orbit around its star, and a value for this is needed to calculate the mass. It has become customary to arbitrarily assume that the plane is exactly lined up with the line of sight from Earth (this produces the lowest possible mass consistent with the spectral line measurements). As Timmy was playing, he saw a man walking towards him. The man looked sad and tired. Timmy's mom always told him to be kind to others, so Timmy went up to the man and said hello. The man smiled and thanked Timmy for being friendly. Timmy felt grateful that he could make someone happy just by saying hello. He remembered the pile of grass he had thrown earlier and had an idea. Title: How to Cut a Hole in an Interior Wall Without Making a Mess Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of cutting a hole in an interior wall without creating a mess. Whether you're installing electrical outlets, plumbing fixtures, or ventilation systems, this step-by-step guide will help ensure a clean and precise job. We'll discuss tools, safety measures, techniques, and post-cut cleanup. List of Required Tools and Materials: 1. Stud finder 2. Tape measure 3. Pencil 4. Level 5. Utility knife 6. Drywall saw 7. Dust mask 8. Safety glasses 9. Drop cloth or plastic sheeting 10. Garbage bag Step 1: Preparation Before starting, clear the area around the work site. Remove any furniture, curtains, or rugs that could get damaged or hamper your movement. Lay down drop cloths or plastic sheets to catch dust and debris. Put on your dust mask and safety glasses to protect yourself from airborne particles and potential eye injury. Key Tip: Use blue painter's tape along the edges of the area where you plan to cut to prevent damaging the surrounding drywall. Step 2: Locate the Studs Use a stud finder to locate the wooden studs within the wall. Mark their positions with a pencil. This is crucial as you don't want to cut into these load-bearing structures. Instead, aim to make your cuts between them. Guideline: Most walls have studs spaced either 16 or 24 inches apart. However, always verify their presence using a stud finder. Step 3: Measure and Mark Your Cutout Decide on the size and shape of the hole needed. Using a level, draw two parallel lines vertically across the wall within the spaces between the studs. Then, mark the top and bottom points of your desired hole width. Connect these marks with straight lines. Repeat at the sides if necessary. Key Tip: If possible, keep the hole size slightly smaller than required; it can always be enlarged later but fixing an oversized hole is more challenging. Step 4: Score the Surface With a utility knife, score along all four lines drawn in Step 3 until you reach the paper layer beneath the outer gypsum board surface. Be careful not to press too hard or deviate from the marked line. Step 5: Start Sawing Insert the blade of a drywall saw into one corner of your scored rectangle. Begin sawing gently along the scored lines, following them closely. Apply steady pressure but avoid forcing the blade. Let its teeth do the work. Step 6: Finish the Cut Continue sawing until you complete the entire perimeter of your outlined hole. Once done, push the loose piece(s) gently towards you. They should come off fairly easily, leaving behind a clean opening. Step 7: Clean Up Vacuum up the dust and small debris left behind. Fold the discarded pieces of drywall neatly before disposing of them in a garbage bag. Carefully remove the protective covering (drop cloth/plastic sheet), ensuring no residual dust spreads around the room. Conclusion: By following these steps meticulously, you've successfully cut a hole in your interior wall with minimal mess. Remember, preparation and precision are key to achieving professional results. Always prioritize safety by wearing appropriate gear and working patiently. [MOT says something to father muffled and then talks baby talk to Lucas [Bro], Chi's noise drowns it out] [audio cuts out in the middle of FAT's sentence, but it's not hard to reconstruct in context.] **Title: 32 Solutions for When You Can't Sleep** Introduction: Lack of sleep can significantly impact your daily life, leading to fatigue, irritability, difficulty concentrating, and even serious health problems. If you're struggling to fall asleep or stay asleep, this comprehensive guide provides 32 evidence-based solutions to help improve your sleep quality. 1. Establish a consistent sleep schedule: Going to bed and waking up at the same time every day stabilizes your circadian rhythm (your body's internal clock), making it easier to fall asleep and wake up feeling rested. Aim for 7-9 hours of sleep per night. 2. Create a restful environment: Make sure your bedroom is dark, quiet, and cool. Consider using blackout curtains, earplugs, or a white noise machine if necessary. Keep technology out of the bedroom as screens emit blue light that interferes with melatonin production. 3. Limit exposure to bright lights before bedtime: Melatonin is a hormone responsible for regulating sleep-wake cycles. Exposure to artificial light from electronic devices suppresses its release, making it harder to fall asleep. Dim lights and avoid screen time two hours prior to bedtime. 4. Practice relaxation techniques: Reducing stress and anxiety through activities like deep breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, meditation, or yoga can promote better sleep. 5. Avoid stimulants close to bedtime: Caffeine, nicotine, and alcohol can disrupt your ability to fall asleep and stay asleep. Refrain from consuming these substances at least four hours before going to bed. 6. Get regular exercise during the day: Physical activity promotes deeper sleep, but try not to engage in intense workouts within three hours of bedtime as they may have the opposite effect. 7. Manage chronic pain: Consult with a healthcare professional about treating underlying conditions causing discomfort, such as arthritis or fibromyalgia, which could be affecting your sleep. 8. Use a comfortable mattress and pillows: Invest in high-quality bedding that supports proper spinal alignment and alleviates pressure points. Replace them every 7-10 years for optimal comfort. 9. Try aromatherapy: Essential oils like lavender, chamomile, and jasmine have calming properties that may aid in promoting better sleep. Diffuse them in your bedroom or add a few drops to your bath before bedtime. 1 It's a wonder you could get out the door. Okay now you make the duck drink.
